content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Introduction
My Greetings to all who are reading this Article. I am Professional Web Experience dev and Web designer and developer. I created the scene of Tesseract from Interstellar Into code through Three js. Here is the Demo of the below project (Note: Click if you are on pc or a good laptop No mobiles) :
https://dotpro-project.netlify.app/
The Idea
About four months ago, I watched Interstellar for the first time. While the entire movie was incredible, the Tesseract scene completely blew my mind. I couldn’t stop thinking about how such an impossible-looking space would feel like in real life?
A few days later, a random idea popped into my head: “What if I built it in Three.js?” At first, I thought it would take a couple of weeks. It ended up taking four months.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Building the Foundation
Before writing any code, I spent some time studying the scene and trying to understand its geometry. I wasn’t aiming for a frame-perfect recreation — I wanted to capture the same feeling.
The first thing I built was a simple plus (+) shape because it was the foundation of the entire structure. I then placed six more around it (one at right, one on left, one on top, one on bottom, one at front and one at back ) to create the first cube-like shape. After grouping them together, I wrote code that repeatedly rendered those groups, creating a larger cube made entirely of smaller cubes built from plus shapes.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Once that was working, I added a player controller so I could actually walk through the environment. By this point, around a month had already passed.
(Hey I don’t want you to think but I know Yes… I’m a pretty lazy coder .)
The Hardest Problem
The next challenge was making the Tesseract feel infinite.
My first attempt was to keep generating more cubes as the player moved. It worked… until the frame rate started decreasing . I spent almost an entire month trying different generation systems, but every solution eventually led to performance issues and lag.
Then I came across a post online (I honestly don’t remember whose it was). The person was solving the same problem (infinite cubes thing) but the situation was different , From that post one idea immediately clicked with me: don’t generate an infinite world….create the illusion of one.
Instead of endlessly spawning cubes, I divided the space into a grid, tracked the player’s position, and moved the Tesseract with the player. The environment felt endless and performance stayed smooth.
Looking back, I spent a month trying to brute-force infinity when all I really needed was a clever solution.
(Yes….. I am dumb don’t think that)
Bringing It to Life
With the generation solved, I moved on to shaders and textures. I spent the remaining time tweaking the visuals until the scene finally had the atmosphere I was aiming for.
When I finally flew through the finished environment, it felt surreal. It wasn’t a perfect recreation of the movie, but it captured the feeling that inspired me to start the project in the first place.
Final Thoughts
This project taught me a lot — not just about Three.js, shaders, or optimization, but about problem-solving. Sometimes the best solution isn’t the most complicated one; it’s simply looking at the problem from a different perspective.
If you’d like to explore the project yourself or get the full source code, you can support my work here:
https://dotprodesigns.gumroad.com/l/efnqal

Thanks for reading, and if you’ve ever had a crazy idea after watching a movie, I’d say give it a shot. You never know where it might take you.
